elements.sent-2411-001: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have achieved impressive results in various vision-language tasks.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, despite showing promising performance, LVLMs suffer from hallucinations caused by language bias, leading to diminished focus on images and ineffective visual comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-003: We identify two primary reasons for this bias: 1).
elements.sent-2411-004: Different scales of training data between the pretraining stage of LLM and multimodal alignment stage.. 2).
elements.sent-2411-005: The learned inference bias due to short-term dependency of text data.
elements.sent-2411-006: Therefore, we propose \OURS, a systemic framework designed to address the language bias of LVLMs with MuLtimodal DuAl-attention MeChanIsm (\MDA) aNd Soft-Image Guidance (\IFG).
elements.sent-2411-007: Specifically, \MDA introduces a parallel dual-attention mechanism that enhances the integration of visual inputs across the model.
elements.sent-2411-008: Then, \IFG further proposes a novel decoding strategy using the soft visual prompt to mitigate the model's over-reliance on adjacent text inputs.
elements.sent-2411-009: Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that our method effectively debiases LVLMs from their language bias, enhancing visual comprehension and reducing hallucinations without requiring additional training resources or data.
elements.sent-2411-010: The code and model are available in \href{https://lacing-lvlm.github.io {lacing-lvlm.github.io .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The emergence of Large Language Models (LLMs)~\citep{openai2023gpt4,touvron2023llama,touvron2023llama2,dubey2024llama3 has marked a significant milestone across various natural language processing tasks~\citep{yang2024gpt4tools,chatgpt .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Leveraging the capabilities of LLMs, the development of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs)~\citep{gpt4o,gpt4v,gemini1,MM1 has been accelerated through the integration of pre-trained visual encoders into these LLMs~\citep{LLAVA,Qwen-VL,chen2023internvl,Mini-Gemini .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This integration allows LVLMs to process both visual and text inputs, and further enables various potential applications such as autonomous driving~\citep{DriveGPT4,mao2023gptdriverlearningdrivegpt,sima2024drivelmdrivinggraphvisual and medical assistants~\citep{li2023llavamedtraininglargelanguageandvision .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Despite their impressive capabilities in handling both two modalities, state-of-the-art LVLMs often produce erroneous or hallucinatory responses irrelevant to the input images~\citep{MMSTAR,lan2024surveyhallucinationlargevisual,HallusionBench .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The main reason behind the hallucinations in LVLMs is referred to as language bias~\citep{MMICL , i.e., these models sometimes ``ignore'' the visual input and generate the text response solely on the text inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, previous studies have not yet thoroughly investigated how this language bias originates.
elements.sent-2411-017: We suggest that this bias potentially emerges for the following two reasons:.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{images/overview_fix.pdf} \caption{Overview of \OURS, a systemic framework, which consists of Multimodal Dual Attention (bottom) and Soft-Image Guidance (above) to mitigate language bias of LVLMs. \MDA proposes a parallel dual-attention mechanism that constructs two separate attention for visual and text inputs during both training and inference. \IFG implements a learnable soft visual prompt during training to replace visual inputs. This soft prompt serves to maintain input patterns while compelling models to prioritize text inputs during inference. } \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-018: Different training scales between the LLM pretraining stage and LVLM alignment stage: The LLM backbone within LVLMs is pre-trained on the large-scale text corpus.
elements.sent-2411-019: Comparatively, the multimodal alignment stage in current SOTA LVLMs utilizes much fewer training samples and shorter training periods.
elements.sent-2411-020: For instance, Llama3~\citep{dubey2024llama3 undergoes pre-training with 15T tokens, whereas the multimodal alignment training for LLaVA~\citep{LLAVA,LLAVA15,liu2024llavanext,Llava-onevision utilizes about 558k to 1.3 million data.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The gap between the training scales of the two stages inevitably makes the pretraining distribution dominate the generation of LVLMs~\citep{llavaBPO , leading to difficulties for LVLMs to fully utilize the visual inputs, e.g., LVLMs often allocate a low attention weight to the visual inputs in more than 90\% layers~\citep{FastV,zhang2024debiasingmultimodallargelanguage .. 2.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: The learned inference bias due to the short-term dependency of text data: Intuitively, a word in a text sequence exhibits a stronger associative bond with adjacent words than those further apart~\citep{alabdulmohsin2024fractalpatternsilluminatesuccess,daniluk2017frustratinglyshortattentionspans,yan2024recurformertransformerheadsneed , i.e., the short-term dependency of text data.}
elements.sent-2411-023: LLMs pre-trained on large-scale text corpora can easily capture and memorize such short-term dependency.
elements.sent-2411-024: Typically, these models assign higher attention weights to adjacent tokens when processing the text data.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, this learned pattern may not be suitable for multi-modal inputs.
elements.sent-2411-026: Visual inputs frequently appear at the beginning of the full inputs in current LVLMs, causing these models to anchor on the nearby text instructions and neglect to draw logical inferences from the actual visual inputs during the text response generation.
elements.sent-2411-027: These two reasons lead to a systemic bias in LVLMs, originating from both training and inference stages.
elements.sent-2411-028: Consequently, a critical question arises: How can we effectively mitigate the language bias inherent in LVLMs from both the training and inference perspectives? Therefore, we propose \OURS, a systemic framework designed to address the language bias of LVLMs with MuLtimodal DuAl-attention MeChanIsm aNd Soft-Image Guidance.
elements.sent-2411-029: Exploring methods to mitigate the dominant influence of the LLM's pretraining distribution~\citep{llavaBPO , which leads to the neglect of visual inputs in most layers~\citep{FastV , remains an area that still requires further exploration.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: To address this issue, we propose the Multimodal Dual-Attention Mechanism (\MDA).
elements.sent-2411-031: Specifically, \MDA first independently calculates attention weights across various modalities, then fuses the two different weights to get the final attention map.
elements.sent-2411-032: It enforces the LVLM to assure considerable attention weights to visual inputs, ensuring integration of visual inputs across all layers.
elements.sent-2411-033: Furthermore, \MDA retains causal attention for text inputs while allowing bidirectional attention to visual inputs.
elements.sent-2411-034: It helps LVLMs preserve the text comprehension ability of LLMs while effectively capturing the global structure of visual inputs, eventually focusing on visual inputs across all layers during training.
elements.sent-2411-035: To mitigate the learned inference bias due to the short-term dependency of text data, we introduce Soft-Image Guidance (\IFG), designed to enhance the visual guidance during LVLMs' response generation.
elements.sent-2411-036: The core idea behind \IFG is to effectively model the current LVLMs’ over-reliance on text inputs, i.e., language bias, and reduce such over-reliance with a novel decoding strategy.
elements.sent-2411-037: Specifically, \IFG first introduces a learnable soft visual prompt to replace the visual input and construct a multimodal-null input for the model.
elements.sent-2411-038: This learnable soft visual prompt serves as a placeholder to maintain the input pattern (e.g., the input length and modalities) and as an indicator, compelling the model to prioritize text inputs.
elements.sent-2411-039: Different from previous works~\citep{vcd,zhang2024debiasingmultimodallargelanguage attempting to model such over-reliance by removing visual inputs or adding random noise on input images, our designed soft visual prompt can maintain the consistent input pattern without introducing uncontrollable informational perturbations.}
elements.sent-2411-040: To get the final soft visual prompt, we replace visual inputs with the soft visual prompt at a given probability during the multimodal alignment stage.
elements.sent-2411-041: The well-learned soft visual prompt is used to replace visual inputs in original inputs to suggest the model prioritize the text input, namely multimodal-null inputs.
elements.sent-2411-042: Finally, we contrast output distributions from original and multimodal-null inputs to get the final output, mitigating the LVLM’s language bias during the response generation.
elements.sent-2411-043: This adjustment ensures that each token in the model's responses accounts for visual input more critically.
elements.sent-2411-044: Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in mitigating language bias in LVLMs, thereby enhancing visual comprehension and reducing visual hallucination.
elements.sent-2411-045: We observe significant improvements, particularly in free-form generation and visual hallucinations reduction (e.g., 11.8-point boost on LLaVA-Bench~\citep{LLAVA and a 40\% improvement on Object Hallucinations Benchmark~\citep{objhal,RLHF-V ).}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Notably, our approach achieves consistent improvement without requiring additional training resources or data beyond standard LVLM multimodal alignment setups~\citep{LLAVA15 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Our analysis further confirms the efficacy of \MDA in enabling the LVLM to fully utilize the visual inputs, as well as the robustness of the \IFG for reducing hallucinations and improving visual comprehension.
elements.sent-2411-048: Despite the impressive capabilities of LVLMs~\citep{gpt4v,gpt4o,gemini1,MM1,gao2024miniInternVL,wang2024qwen2vl,Llava-onevision , these models still struggle with generating responses irrelevant to the input images~\citep{lan2024surveyhallucinationlargevisual,liu2024surveyhallucinationlargevisionlanguage , e.g., falsely perceiving the presence of non-existent objects in images~\citep{zhou2024analyzingmitigatingobjecthallucination .}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Moreover, \citet{zhang2024debiasingmultimodallargelanguage note that LVLMs tend to allocate more attention to text inputs and increasingly prioritize them as response generation progresses.}
elements.sent-2411-050: These works indicate that LVLMs often allocate a low attention weight to the visual inputs, thus limiting the use of the given image.
elements.sent-2411-051: Consequently, solutions for mitigating such bias remain a crucial problem and means to further improve the performance of LVLMs.
elements.sent-2411-052: We argue that there are two main reasons that lead to the language bias in LVLMs, including (1) the different training scales between the pretraining stage and multimodal alignment stage, and (2) the learned inference bias due to the short-term dependency of text data.
elements.sent-2411-053: We further propose a systematic method \OURS to address the language bias from both the training and inference perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-054: Given the language bias within current LVLMs, LVLMs exhibit similar hallucination problems as the textual LLMs~\citep{llmHall .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Moreover, LVLMs show unique types of hallucination, such as object hallucination~\citep{objhal,pope , i.e., LVLMs tend to encompass content not supported by associated images.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Recently, many methods have been proposed to mitigate the hallucination in LVLMs.
elements.sent-2411-057: LRV~\citep{lrv attempts to apply supervised fine-tuning on a well-designed visual preference dataset to address this issue.}
elements.sent-2411-058: LLaVA-BPO~\citep{llavaBPO further proposes the pipeline to gather preference datasets and conduct preference learning to mitigate this type of hallucination.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Additionally, LLaVA-RLHF~\citep{LLAVA-RLHF and RLHF-V~\citep{RLHF-V introduce reinforcement learning from human feedback into the LVLM training pipeline to align outputs with human intentions, thereby reducing hallucinations.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: However, these methods typically necessitate a substantial number of additional training data and computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-061: Thus, many methods such as VCD~\citep{vcd , IBD~\citep{IBD , VDD~\citep{zhang2024debiasingmultimodallargelanguage , and ICD~\citep{ICD introduce training-free methods that contrast model outputs generated from original inputs and inputs without visual inputs (or inputs with masked visual inputs) to reduce the influence of textual LLMs.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Nonetheless, these methods may introduce unexpected information during inference that was not present during training.
elements.sent-2411-063: This inconsistency between the inputs at the training and inference stages can result in suboptimal performance of these methods.
elements.sent-2411-064: Different from the two types of methods, our method has two notable strengths: (1) We propose our new method in a systematic way, addressing the language bias from both the training and inference perspectives without requiring additional training data; (2) Different from these methods only consider a part influence of language bias, e.g., object hallucination, our method addresses the language bias in two perspectives that further improve the performance of LVLMS in more general tasks.
elements.sent-2411-065: In this section, we present our approach for mitigating the language bias inherent in LVLMs using two complementary methods: the Multimodal Dual-Attention Mechanism and Soft-Image Guidance.
elements.sent-2411-066: These method address the language bias of LVLMs from both training and inference perspectives, each targeting distinct issues.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/average_attention_scores.pdf} \caption{Average attention scores for output tokens towards text and visual tokens across different layers in LLaVA-1.5~\citep{LLAVA15}, showing that only the first two layers apply considerable attention to visual tokens. In contrast, deeper layers largely neglect them.} \label{fig:avg_attention}
elements.sent-2411-067: Most LVLMs project bidirectional visual inputs into the unidirectional LLM space using a relatively small amount of multimodal data~\citep{LLAVA,LLAVA15,Llava-onevision,zhu2023minigpt4enhancingvisionlanguageunderstanding compared to vast data scales employed in pretraining LLMs~\citep{dubey2024llama3,openai2023gpt4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-068: These models treat visual inputs as a different form of text inputs in an autoregressive manner.
elements.sent-2411-069: The mismatch in both modeling and training scale leads LVLMs to partially adapt to data distribution changes using only shallow layers during training with limited data~\citep{zhang2024debiasingmultimodallargelanguage .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Consequently, the model remains dominated by the LLM's pretraining distribution and lacks effective attention to visual inputs in deeper layers.
elements.sent-2411-071: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:avg_attention , LVLMs exhibit considerable attention toward visual inputs only in the first two layers~\citep{FastV , while deeper layers retain their original distributions, causing deeper layers of LVLMs to ignore visual inputs.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: To address this issue, we propose Multimodal Dual-Attention Mechanism (\MDA), which introduces a parallel dual-attention mechanism that preserves separate attention metrics for visual and text inputs in the LVLMs.
elements.sent-2411-073: It enforces the LLM to allocate sufficient attention toward visual inputs and encourages LVLMs to fully leverage their LLM backbone for visual comprehension during training.
elements.sent-2411-074: Specifically, \MDA is parallel dual-attention strategy that retain unidirectional, causal attention for text inputs while allowing bidirectional attention towards visual inputs for comprehensive understanding.
elements.sent-2411-075: As illustrated in Equation~\ref{eq:multimodal_attn , Given the multimodal input $\mathbf{S = \langle s_1, s_2, \dots, s_N \rangle$, $s_n$ means the token in the input.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: To independently calculate attention weights across two modalities, we define two attention masks: visual token mask $\mathbf{M _{\mathcal{I }$ for visual tokens $\mathcal{I}$ and text token mask $\mathbf{M _{\mathcal{T }$ for text tokens $\mathcal{T}$: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{M}_{\mathcal{I}}[i, j] = \begin{cases} 1, \text{if } s_j \in \mathcal{I}, 0, \text{otherwise}, \end{cases} \mathbf{M}_{\mathcal{T}}[i, j] = \begin{cases} 1, \text{if } s_j \in \mathcal{T} \And i \leq j, 0, \text{otherwise}, \end{cases} \end{aligned} \label{eq:multimodal_attn}\end{equation} We can use designed attention masks to calculate attention weights of visual$(\mathbf{W _{\mathcal{I )$ and test tokens$(\mathbf{W _{\mathcal{T )$: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{W}_{\mathcal{I}} = \text{softmax}\left(\mathbf{Q} \mathbf{K}^\top / \sqrt{d_k} \odot \mathbf{M}_{\mathcal{I}} \right), \mathbf{W}_{\mathcal{T}} = \text{softmax}\left( \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{K}^\top / \sqrt{d_k} \odot \mathbf{M}_{\mathcal{T}} \right), \end{aligned} \label{eq:attn_Wcal}\end{equation} where the $\mathbf{Q ,\mathbf{K}$ represent the query, key and in the self-attention mechanism of LVLMs.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation} M_{I}[i, j] =  1, if  s_j \in I, 0, otherwise,  M_{T}[i, j] =  1, if  s_j \in T \And i \leq j, 0, otherwise,   \label{eq:multimodal_attn}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation} W_{I} = softmax\left(Q K^\top / \sqrt{d_k} \odot M_{I} \right), W_{T} = softmax\left( Q K^\top / \sqrt{d_k} \odot M_{T} \right),  \label{eq:attn_Wcal}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-077: Finally, the two attention weights, denoted as $(\mathbf{W _{\mathcal{I )$ and $(\mathbf{W _{\mathcal{T )$, are integrated and subsequently multiplied by $\mathbf{V}$, the value in the attention mechanism of LVLMs, to derive the final attention score $\mathbf{A}$ based on \MDA. \begin{equation}\mathbf{A} = \left( \mathbf{W}_{\mathcal{I}} + \mathbf{W}_{\mathcal{T}} \right) \mathbf{V}. \label{eq:attn}\end{equation} Parallel computation of attention weights guarantees each token separately receives attention from both visual and text inputs, balancing their contributions.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: It allows visual inputs to remain relevance across all layers, avoiding shallow adaptation and language bias.
elements.sent-2411-079: The dual-attention enhances the visual understanding of LVLMs while preserving its language modeling capabilities.

elements.sent-2411-080: A significant challenge in visual comprehension with LVLMs stems from their inference bias due to the short-term dependency of text data.
elements.sent-2411-081: Due to the sequential nature of language modeling—which prioritizes coherence and continuity—LVLMs tend to focus on nearby text tokens, often at the expense of the visual information that may be distant or disparate, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:avg_attnntion_by_tokens .}
elements.sent-2411-082: To mitigate the inference bias of LVLMs, we propose the Soft-Image Guidance (\IFG), designed to enhance the guidance of visual inputs during LVLMs’ response generation.

Attention to visual tokens (a) decreases as response generation progresses, while attention to text tokens (b) increases.} \label{fig:avg_attnntion_by_tokens}
elements.sent-2411-083: To enhance the guidance of visual inputs in LVLMs, we formulate the visual comprehension mathematically.
elements.sent-2411-084: We consider the conditional probability $p(y_t\mid v)$ of generating a response token \( y_t \) given the visual input \( v \).
elements.sent-2411-085: By applying Bayes' theorem, we have: \begin{equation}\label{eq:conditional_generation} p(y_t \mid v) = \frac{p(v \mid y_t) \cdot p(y_t)}{p(v)}\end{equation} Then we take the logarithm of both sides of Equation~\eqref{eq:conditional_generation : \begin{equation}\label{eq:log_conditional_generation} \log p(y_t \mid v) = \log p(v \mid y_t) + \log p(y_t) - \log p(v)\end{equation} In Equation~\eqref{eq:log_conditional_generation , \( p(y_t) \) is the unconditional probability of generating the target token \( y_t \) without any visual input.}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eq:log_conditional_generation} \log p(y_t \mid v) = \log p(v \mid y_t) + \log p(y_t) - \log p(v)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-086: To amplify the influence of the visual input \( v \) on response generation, we introduce a scaling parameter \( \lambda \) for conditional probability \( p(v \mid y_t) \).
elements.sent-2411-087: We adjust the \( p(v \mid y_t) \) to obtain an enhanced version \( \widehat{p (y_t \mid v) \), defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:scaled_log_likelihood} \log \widehat{p}(y_t \mid v) \propto \lambda \cdot \log p(v \mid y_t) + \log p(y_t) - \log p(v)\end{equation} Our goal is to express \( \log \widehat{p (y_t \mid v) \) with known quantities.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: To this end, we expand \( \log p(v \mid y_t) \) using Bayes' theorem: \begin{equation}\label{eq:log_p_v_given_yt} \log p(v \mid y_t) = \log p(y_t \mid v) + \log p(v) - \log p(y_t)\end{equation} Substituting Equation~\eqref{eq:log_p_v_given_yt into Equation~\eqref{eq:scaled_log_likelihood , we obtain: \begin{equation}\label{eq:expanded_log_likelihood} \begin{aligned} \log \widehat{p}(y_t \mid v) \propto \lambda \left( \log p(y_t \mid v) + \log p(v) - \log p(y_t) \right) \quad + \log p(y_t) - \log p(v). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Since \( v \) is given (fixed), \(\log p(v)\) is a constant with respect to \(y_t\) and can be omitted, we simplify Equation~\eqref{eq:expanded_log_likelihood to: \begin{equation}\label{eq:simplified_log_likelihood} \log \widehat{p}(y_t \mid v) \propto \lambda (\log p(y_t \mid v) - \log p(y_t) ) + \log p(y_t)\end{equation} Equation~\eqref{eq:expanded_log_likelihood demonstrates that the influence of visual input \( v \) on response generation can be amplified by adjusting the scaling parameter \( \lambda \), once given the conditional probability \( p(y_t \mid v) \) of original inputs and the unconditional probability \( p(y_t) \) without visual inputs.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{eq:log_p_v_given_yt} \log p(v \mid y_t) = \log p(y_t \mid v) + \log p(v) - \log p(y_t)\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\label{eq:expanded_log_likelihood}  \log \widehat{p}(y_t \mid v) \propto \lambda \left( \log p(y_t \mid v) + \log p(v) - \log p(y_t) \right) \quad + \log p(y_t) - \log p(v). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-089: This formulation highlights a major challenge in enhancing visual guidance for LVLMs: accurately calculating the unconditional probability \( p(y_t) \) of generating the token \( y_t \) in the absence of the visual input.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Joint Training of LVLM with \IFG } \label{alg:image_free_guidance} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require $P$: Model; $\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{V}$: Training dataset \Repeat \State $(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{v}) \sim (\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{V})$ \Comment{Sample multimodal input data} \State $\mathbf{v} \gets \epsilon$ with probability $\theta$ \Comment{Randomly discard visual input, replace with learnable soft visual prompt $\epsilon$} \State $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cross-entropy}} = -\mathbb{E}_{(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{v})} \sum\limits_{i} \mathbf{y}_i \log P(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{v})$ \State Update $P$ and $\epsilon$ \Until{converged} \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-094: Therefore, \IFG first employs a learnable soft visual prompt \( \epsilon \) to replace visual inputs, thereby forming a multimodal-null input for the model.
elements.sent-2411-095: The learnable soft visual prompt \( \epsilon \) will be the jointly trained with the LVLM.
elements.sent-2411-096: As outlined in Algorithm~\ref{alg:image_free_guidance , we replace visual input with \( \epsilon \) with probability \( \theta \) during training.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The soft visual prompt \( \epsilon \) serves dual purposes, acting both as a placeholder to maintain input patterns and as an indicator to make model prioritize text inputs.
elements.sent-2411-098: This dual functionality ensures a consistent input pattern for LVLMs in both training and inference, allowing the model to produce generate interpretable output and balancing visual and text inputs.
elements.sent-2411-099: After training, we can directly use the \( \epsilon \) to query the model and extract the approximation of \( p(y_t) \).
elements.sent-2411-100: Finally, during inference, we contrast output distributions from original and multimodal-null inputs based on Equation~\ref{eq:simplified_log_likelihood to get the final output.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Specifically, logits \( \ell_{g \) of generated tokens are recalculated by adjusting the logits \( \ell_{u \) of the multimodal-null inputs with the scaling parameter \( \lambda \), based on logits \( \ell_{c \) of original inputs as follows: \begin{equation}\ell_{g} = \ell_{u} + (\ell_{c} - \ell_{u}) \times \lambda\end{equation} This refinement facilitates a more balanced and effective integration of visual inputs, thereby enhancing visual comprehension of LVLMs while addressing the language bias.}}}

elements.sent-2411-156: This paper tackles the language bias in LVLMs, which often leads to the neglect of visual inputs and the generation of hallucinatory responses.
elements.sent-2411-157: We identify two primary sources of this bias: the gap in training scales between the pretraining and multimodal alignment stages of LVLMs, and the learned inference bias due to the short-term dependency in text data.
elements.sent-2411-158: To address these challenges, we introduced the Multimodal Dual-Attention Mechanism (\MDA) and Soft-Image Guidance (\IFG).
elements.sent-2411-159: Our work highlights the importance of addressing language biases from both training and inference perspectives, paving the way for more accurate and reliable LVLMs.

elements.sent-2411-170: Despite the promising results demonstrated by \OURS in addressing the language bias of LVLMs, several limitations must be acknowledged.
elements.sent-2411-171: First, our method is mainly conducted on the LLaVA-Family model~\citep{LLAVA,LLAVA15 .}
elements.sent-2411-172: The effectiveness of our approach on other LVLMs with different architectures remains largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-173: Nevertheless, language bias is commonly observed across various LVLMs~\citep{MMICL,MMSTAR,FastV .}
elements.sent-2411-174: Both the Q-former-based and Resampler-based methods~\citep{Qwen-VL are susceptible to language bias, exhibiting several phenomena similar to those seen in the LLaVA model~\citep{FastV .}}
elements.sent-2411-175: Therefore, inspired by this common motivation, we anticipate the implementation and effectiveness of \OURS on diverse LVLMs.
elements.sent-2411-176: Additionally, due to resource constraints, we are unable to acquire LVLMs that achieve a similar scale of training between the LLM pretraining stages and the LVLM alignment stage to accurately validate the source of language bias.
elements.sent-2411-177: Finally, while \OURS has significantly reduced hallucinations in LVLMs and enhanced visual comprehension capabilities, there remains a possibility for it to produce hallucinations or disseminate misinformation.
elements.sent-2411-178: Therefore, it still should be employed with caution in critical applications.
elements.sent-2411-179: Consequently, future research could involve broadening our approach to include a wider spectrum of LVLMs with different architectures and training them using a comparable training scale to observe the manifestations of language bias.
elements.sent-2411-180: To make fair compression, we adopt the same training settings as LLaVA-1.5~\citep{LLAVA15 , maintaining consistency in hyperparameters, training dataset, data preprocessing, and model architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-181: The only differences lies in the introduction of the multimodal dual-attention mechanism and the learnable soft visual prompt for soft-image guidance.
elements.sent-2411-182: Following the setting of LLaVA-1.5~\citep{LLAVA15 , we employ CLIP-ViT-L-14-336~\citep{radford2021clip as the visual encoder, paired with a two-layer MLP adapter to project visual embeddings from the encoder to the LLM backbone.}}

elements.sent-2411-186: In addition to these standard components of LLaVA-1.5, our method includes two significant modifications to the model architecture.
elements.sent-2411-187: Firstly, we adopt the multimodal dual-attention mechanism proposed in this paper, replacing the vanilla self-attention in the LLM.
